Conversely, banners serving purposes unrelated to elections, such as "welcome" messages or "festive greetings," are unlikely to constitute election advertising. The determination depends on the specific context, intention, and timing of the display rather than a blanket prohibition for all GRAs running for office.

results.title: Update on Generating Electricity for Pulau Ubin Residents Using Micro-grid
results.summary: The Pulau Ubin micro-grid, operated by EDP Renewables since 2013, currently serves over 30 consumers at a rate of 80 cents per kWh, which is lower than diesel generator costs but higher than the regulated SP tariff. The Energy Market Authority (EMA) and developers are reviewing tariffs as newer technologies may reduce generation costs, though no specific timeline for adjustment or mechanism for triggering reviews was stated. Financial assistance is available for vulnerable households, while the Government noted that Pulau Ubin residents face different cost structures, such as no Certificate of Entitlement fees or water bills, making direct tariff equalisation difficult. The arrangement's exit clause and outage compensation mechanisms were not addressed in the response.

results.title: Update on Singapore's Progress in Achieving "30 by 30" Food Sustainability Goal in 2030
results.summary: Singapore is currently producing less than 10% of its food locally, with the "30 by 30" goal remaining aspirational due to post-pandemic supply uncertainties and hurdles in scaling alternative proteins caused by high energy costs and low consumer acceptance. Despite these challenges, local production of hen eggs and beansprouts now exceeds 30% and 50% of consumption respectively, supported by the Singapore Food Agency's initiatives including the National Broodstock Centre, Hatchery Development and Recognition Programme, and enhanced Agri-Food Cluster Transformation Fund. The Ministry is reviewing local production targets as part of its overall food supply resilience strategy to address specific challenges and foster sector growth, with further details on updated policies to be shared when ready.

results.title: Update on Water Seepage Cases in HDB Flats and Measures to Expedite Repairs
results.summary: From January to August 2025, HDB assisted with 615 complex water seepage cases, averaging 77 monthly cases compared to 72 in 2024, with no observed surge during monsoon seasons. The average age of flats with reported ceiling leaks is approximately 40 years. While flat owners maintain interior repairs, HDB co-pays 50% of costs via the Goodwill Repair Assistance scheme, with the remaining 50% shared equally between upper and lower floor owners. For serious inter-floor leaks, HDB expedites repairs contingent on upper-floor availability and may conduct temporary lower-floor repairs. External wall seepage cases remain under Town Council purview, supported by HDB through joint inspections and technical advice. HDB is also studying microwave scanning technology to better diagnose seepage sources. Residents requiring financial assistance are referred to grassroots organisations and Community Development Councils.

results.title: Updating Official Records and School Textbooks in Light of Declassified Singapore Independence Documents
results.summary: The Minister for Digital Development and Information clarified that no updates to official records or school textbooks regarding Singapore's independence from Malaysia are currently planned. She noted that the declassified documents referenced in a recent CNA documentary were already incorporated into the 1998 publication "Singapore in Malaysia and the Politics of Disengagement" and Lee Kuan Yew's memoir, "The Singapore Story." The Minister explained that these historical accounts reflect differing points of view, such as describing independence as a "negotiated separation," and that students with access to these materials can form their own nuanced views. Consequently, the Government has not provided a specific timeline for further updates to the national narrative on this period.

results.title: Usage of Mindline.sg and Effectiveness in Linking Users to Appropriate Care beyond Digital Engagement
results.summary: Between 2022 and 2025, unique monthly visitors to Mindline.sg rose 82% to over 50,000, with increased page exploration and session duration. Since its June 2020 launch, the platform facilitated over 206,000 self-assessments and 795,000 mental health exercises. User demographics and health outcomes remain untracked due to anonymity, while effectiveness is assessed via periodic surveys. More than 80% of Wysa chatbot users found cognitive exercises helpful, and over 170 individuals with diagnosed psychiatric conditions reported high satisfaction, particularly those with prior admissions.

results.title: Vehicles Approved for Registration Under Voluntary Welfare Organisations
results.summary: As of 18 September 2025, 274 vehicles remain registered under voluntary welfare organisations via the Certificate of Entitlement (COE) Grant Scheme, which replaced the COE Exemption Scheme in 2018. This scheme covers COE and Additional Registration Fee costs for eligible social service agencies transporting vulnerable persons, such as seniors or persons with disabilities, to access key services. Agencies requiring vehicles for this purpose may apply for the scheme. The Acting Minister for Transport confirmed these figures and eligibility criteria during the parliamentary debate.

results.title: Welfare and Benefits Assurance for Seniors Residing in Group Homes
results.summary: Seniors in MSF-funded group homes without individual addresses are currently excluded from household-based CDC Vouchers, which are allocated per household. To resolve this, MSF is collaborating with the People's Association and homes to facilitate voucher collection at nearby community centres for eligible residents. The Ministry expects to fully implement this access arrangement by October 2025 and will continue assisting residents in accessing other welfare benefits and support measures.

results.title: President's Speech
results.summary: The Government proposes a new, government-funded "AI Traineeship" programme to address rising unemployment and job insecurity among young Singaporeans amid economic uncertainty and AI disruption. Distinct from existing graduate traineeships, this scheme aims to catalyse SME business transformation by deploying young, AI-trained workers as "AI Change Agents" to help firms automate processes and strengthen economic competitiveness. While the Government will continue to review the BTO income ceiling and supply affordable housing, it emphasizes that skills training alone is insufficient without a deep local industrial ecosystem to defend workers against geopolitical risks. The President's Address further outlines a strategy to break intergenerational disadvantage and define success by enabling the next generation to do better, ensuring economic growth translates into a fairer society where progress is shared through the "we first" principle.
